title: KMeans._init_random_centroids can pick the same sample for two different centroids
body: In mlfromscratch/unsupervised_learning/k_means.py, _init_random_centroids() calls np.random.choice(range(n_samples)) independently for each of the k centroids with no exclusion of already-picked indices, so for small k or small datasets two centroids can start out identical, wasting an iteration and sometimes producing an empty cluster.

title: KMeans._calculate_centroids produces NaN centroid when a cluster becomes empty
body: _calculate_centroids() in k_means.py does np.mean(X[cluster], axis=0) for each cluster; if _create_clusters() ever assigns zero samples to a cluster (possible after a bad centroid initialization), X[cluster] is an empty array and np.mean returns NaN, which then propagates through every subsequent _closest_centroid call and silently corrupts the fit.

title: LogisticRegression batch optimization mode (gradient_descent=False) has no convergence check
body: LogisticRegression.fit() in supervised_learning/logistic_regression.py always runs the full n_iterations loop even in the gradient_descent=False branch, which uses np.linalg.pinv for batch Newton-like updates and typically converges in a handful of iterations; running 4000 iterations of pinv on large X is extremely slow with no early-stopping/tolerance option.

title: LogisticRegression.predict does not expose predicted probabilities, only rounded classes
body: predict() in logistic_regression.py returns np.round(self.sigmoid(...)).astype(int), discarding the raw probability. Several examples (e.g. mlfromscratch/examples/logistic_regression.py) would benefit from a predict_proba-style method for plotting ROC curves or confidence thresholds.

title: activation_functions.Sigmoid has no numerical stability guard for large negative inputs
body: mlfromscratch/deep_learning/activation_functions.py's Sigmoid presumably computes 1 / (1 + np.exp(-x)) directly; for very negative x (e.g. during early unstable logistic_regression.py training with a high learning_rate), np.exp(-x) overflows to inf and produces RuntimeWarning noise instead of clamping to 0.
